Requires the Secretary of Transportation to review and report to Congress within roughly 15 months on how airlines categorize flight delays and cancellations, particularly whether weather attributions are accurate and whether they disproportionately affect smaller communities or mislead passengers.

Requires the Secretary of Transportation to review within one year whether it is an unfair or deceptive practice for an airline to significantly change a passenger's itinerary more than 24 hours before departure without offering compensation or better alternatives, and to report findings to Congress within 90 days of completing the review.

Expands family assistance obligations for air carriers and the NTSB after aircraft accidents by removing size and severity thresholds so that the requirements apply to any accident with any loss of life for which the NTSB is the lead investigative agency, and extends protections to ground victims.

Directs the Government Accountability Office to study and report to Congress within one year on airport accessibility best practices for people with disabilities that go beyond what existing disability laws already require.

Extends the Advisory Committee for Aviation Consumer Protection through September 30, 2021, requires non-government members to annually disclose financial conflicts of interest, and mandates that the committee's annual recommendations be posted on the Department of Transportation website.
Extends the deadline for airports to submit competitive access reports from October 1, 2017 to October 1, 2021.

Requires the Secretary of Transportation to issue regulations within one year mandating that airlines automatically refund passengers for any ancillary fees paid for services they did not actually receive.

Requires the Secretary of Transportation to issue regulations within one year mandating that airlines and ticket agents disclose key fees—such as baggage, cancellation, change, ticketing, and seat selection fees—to consumers in a standardized format before purchase, whether online or by phone.

Requires airlines and ticket agents to inform consumers, within 15 months, that paid seat selection is optional and that a free seat will be assigned if no seat is purchased, with this disclosure made online, by phone, at check-in, and at other pre-departure opportunities.

Directs the Secretary of Transportation to review airline policies on traveling while pregnant within 180 days and, if appropriate, issue regulations requiring airlines to offer pre-boarding to pregnant passengers who request it.

Amends federal law to require airlines, foreign carriers, and ticket agents to inform consumers at the point of sale and on their websites about how to file service complaints both with the carrier and with the Department of Transportation's Aviation Consumer Protection Division, and directs the Secretary to issue implementing regulations within one year.

Establishes an Aviation Consumer Advocate within the Department of Transportation's Aviation Consumer Protection Division to help resolve passenger complaints, evaluate complaint handling, and recommend enforcement and regulatory improvements, with annual reporting to Congress.

Requires the Secretary of Transportation to evaluate and improve the aviation consumer protection section of the DOT website within 180 days and to develop and release, within one year, a free mobile app that lets travelers access passenger rights information and file aviation consumer complaints.

Directs the Architectural and Transportation Barriers Compliance Board, in consultation with the DOT and relevant stakeholders, to conduct a study within two years on how passengers with significant disabilities who use wheelchairs—including power wheelchairs—can be safely accommodated through in-cabin wheelchair restraint systems.

Establishes an advisory committee to advise the Secretary of Transportation on improving air travel for passengers with disabilities, including identifying access barriers, evaluating DOT programs, and submitting annual reports with recommendations.

Requires the Secretary of Transportation, when developing best practices for assisting passengers with disabilities, to include specific recommendations on addressing air carrier failures to provide wheelchair assistance and how carrier training programs can correct those failures.

Requires the Secretary of Transportation to review and update regulations within 180 days to ensure airports and airlines provide timely, effective assistance to passengers with disabilities, and mandates annual hands-on training for all personnel who physically assist those passengers.

Increases civil penalties up to three times the normal maximum for airline violations that injure a passenger with a disability or damage their wheelchair or mobility aid, and makes each individual act of discrimination a separate punishable offense.

Directs the Secretary of Transportation to create a plain-language 'Airline Passengers with Disabilities Bill of Rights' document summarizing passenger rights and carrier responsibilities, which airlines must post on their websites and include in pre-flight communications to passengers who disclose a disability.

Requires the Government Accountability Office to study and report to Congress within one year on how the Department of Transportation enforces aviation consumer protection rules, including available enforcement tools, obstacles, and enforcement trends.

Directs the FAA Administrator to begin a study within 18 months on establishing minimum seat pitch (legroom) standards for passenger aircraft, with particular attention to passenger safety including for those with disabilities.

Requires the Secretary of Transportation to issue regulations banning passengers from making voice calls on mobile phones during flights, with exceptions for flight crew, flight attendants, and federal law enforcement officers acting in an official capacity.

Prohibits airlines from removing a passenger who has already been cleared and boarded without the passenger's consent except for safety, security, health, or behavioral reasons; eliminates dollar caps on involuntary denied-boarding compensation; and requires the GAO to study airline oversale practices.

Sets January 1, 2018 as the effective compliance date for the existing federal rule requiring airlines to report data on mishandled wheelchairs and other mobility aids transported in aircraft cargo compartments.

Requires the GAO to report to Congress within 180 days on the availability and accessibility of lavatories on commercial aircraft, including accessibility for passengers with disabilities, safety concerns from shrinking lavatory sizes, and complaint trends.

Requires the GAO to report on airline employee and contractor training policies regarding racial, ethnic, and religious nondiscrimination, and directs the Secretary of Transportation to develop and distribute best practices to airlines based on those findings.

Requires the Secretary of Transportation to issue a final rule within 90 days establishing minimum customer service standards for large ticket agents (those with $100 million or more in annual revenue) to ensure consumer protections comparable to those required of airlines.

Expresses Congress's opinion that the aviation industry and all stakeholders must ensure equal access to air travel for individuals with all types of disabilities at every airport and across all airline services as technology advances.

Directs the Secretary of Transportation to issue regulations within 270 days prohibiting airlines from charging fees—including for cancellations, baggage, seat selection, and same-day flight changes—that are unreasonable or disproportionate to the airline's actual costs.

Extends and increases funding for the Essential Air Service program to $175 million per year for fiscal years 2018 through 2021, updates eligibility definitions, and allows the Secretary to consider seasonal flexibility when awarding service contracts at seasonal airports.

Increases annual funding for the Small Community Air Service Development Program to $10 million per year through 2021 and expands eligibility to include any airport no larger than a small hub that has insufficient air service or unreasonably high fares.

Allows the Secretary to waive the rule against funding the same project twice if the community previously spent little money or faced uncontrollable industry or environmental challenges, and permits the Secretary to amend or narrow the scope of a small community air service grant agreement at the request of the community and carrier.

Allows eligible small communities to request that the Secretary waive, in whole or in part, the aircraft size and service-level requirements for Essential Air Service eligibility, with the waiver lasting a limited time period.
Defines key terms—including Administration, Administrator, ADS-B, ADS-B Out, and NextGen—used throughout Title IV of the bill.

Requires the FAA Administrator to submit annual reports to Congress on each NextGen program's return on investment, expected completion, user benefits, and safety contributions, and to maintain a prioritized list of NextGen programs until each achieves a positive return on investment.

Directs the FAA Administrator to make a final investment decision by September 30, 2018, on whether to implement a reduced oceanic separation capability that, if justified by a positive business case, would be operational by the end of 2020.

Requires the FAA to establish annual measurable performance goals for NextGen, include progress toward those goals in required reports, and tie the Chief NextGen Officer's bonus evaluations and organizational agreements to quantifiable NextGen efficiency, productivity, capacity, and safety objectives.

Requires the FAA Administrator to update air traffic control contingency plans within one year and every five years thereafter, report to Congress on those updates, and convene NextGen officials within 180 days to assess how NextGen capabilities can improve national airspace resilience against facility outages like the 2014 Chicago Center fire.

Directs the FAA, working with the NextGen Advisory Committee, to identify barriers to the 2020 ADS-B Out equipment mandate, develop and submit a plan to address those barriers, and report annually to Congress on compliance progress until January 1, 2020.

Requires the FAA Administrator to conduct a gap analysis of risks to NextGen interoperability with foreign air navigation service providers, develop a mitigation plan, and report findings and actions to Congress within one year.
Directs the FAA Administrator to identify technical and operational gaps in NextGen transition plans, develop a mitigation plan, and report to Congress on actions taken within one year.

Requires the FAA to track use of performance-based navigation procedures, identify additional locations and opportunities for implementation, engage stakeholders early, and report annually to Congress on progress and plans for expanding PBN at additional airports.

Directs the FAA Administrator to consider revising airworthiness certification regulations to address cybersecurity for avionics systems and to require that flight-critical avionics be secured against unauthorized access through passenger in-flight entertainment systems.

Requires the Government Accountability Office to assess whether items in the FAA's NextGen budget actually contribute to a safer and more efficient air traffic control system and to recommend removal of any line items that do not, reporting findings to Congress within one year.

Requires the FAA Administrator to incorporate human factors analysis in the early design phases of all NextGen programs and to include a dedicated human factors specialist in the NextGen approval process, with a progress report to Congress within one year.

Directs the FAA Administrator to review and improve acquisition cost-reporting practices to ensure accurate tracking of total system costs, and to report to Congress on progress within one year and every two years thereafter.
Requires the FAA Administrator to provide cost-benefit statements and a joint investment schedule before mandating that airspace users adopt NextGen-related equipment.

Directs the FAA Administrator to identify workforce barriers, develop a recruitment and retention plan for technical fields like cybersecurity and systems engineering, and report progress to Congress within one year.
Requires the FAA Administrator to consult statistical specialists to identify and prioritize NextGen implementation risks, develop a mitigation method, and report to Congress within one year.

Directs the FAA Administrator, working with the NextGen Advisory Committee and National Academies, to identify best practices, assess key risks for major NextGen programs, develop mitigation plans, and report findings to Congress within one year.

Requires the FAA Administrator to report to Congress within one year on the system-wide benefits of NextGen programs, including reductions in delays, increased throughput, lower emissions, and improved safety.

Requires the FAA Administrator to report to Congress within one year identifying the top five research priorities for NextGen implementation, explaining why each is a priority, who is assisting with the research, and when it will be completed.

Requires the FAA Administrator to submit annual reports to Congress on the status of NextGen implementation at commercial service airports, including developing a standard metric for measuring percentage completion.
Directs the FAA Administrator to identify and implement agency-wide cost-saving initiatives with measurable schedules and metrics, and to report progress to Congress within one year.

Requires the Secretary of Transportation to establish performance measures and targets for FAA administration within 180 days, and directs the DOT Inspector General to report to Congress on progress toward meeting those targets within two years.

Allows the FAA Administrator to furlough non-essential employees to meet required spending reductions while protecting employees whose work involves human safety or property protection, and permits internal budget transfers to maintain essential staff.

Requires the FAA to conduct an in-person interview with every air traffic control specialist applicant before hiring, with guidelines established within 30 days and the requirement in effect within 60 days of enactment.

Requires the FAA Administrator to submit a report to Congress within 90 days detailing staffing and scheduling plans for air traffic control facilities in the New York City and Newark region for the following year.
Requires the FAA Administrator to develop and publish in the Federal Register a work plan for the New York/New Jersey/Philadelphia Metropolitan Area Airspace Project within 90 days of enactment.
Requires the FAA to provide air traffic services and aviation safety support for airshows and fly-ins at no charge to event organizers, funded from existing FAA appropriations.

Requires the FAA Administrator to publish annual reports for five years on the impact of disabled veteran leave provisions on the FAA workforce and the number of disabled veterans benefitting from that leave.
Extends veterans' preference hiring protections to Federal Aviation Administration employees by applying additional statutory provisions to the FAA's personnel management system.

Requires the FAA Administrator to designate Aircraft Registry Office employees in Oklahoma City as excepted (essential) workers who must continue working during any government shutdown or emergency furlough.
Removes a specific subsection from the law governing National Transportation Safety Board investigative officers.
Clarifies that air tour operators flying over federal lands near Grand Canyon National Park solely as a transportation route—including over Hoover Dam—are exempt from overflight regulations.

Requires the FAA to consider the impact on commercial space launch and reentry vehicles when conducting aeronautical studies of proposed structures, and mandates a rulemaking within 18 months to implement this change.

Directs the Secretaries of Transportation and Health and Human Services, in coordination with other federal agencies, to develop a comprehensive national aviation communicable disease preparedness plan within one year.

Codifies the continued operation of the FAA's Advanced Materials Center of Excellence to conduct applied research on durable aircraft materials and authorizes $500,000 annually for fiscal years 2018 and 2021.

Requires the GAO to study violence against airline customer service employees and directs air carriers to develop and submit employee assault prevention and response plans based on the study's findings.
Requires the FAA to mandate installation of secondary cockpit security barriers on all new aircraft manufactured for U.S. passenger air carriers within one year of enactment.

Directs the FAA to fund research, training, demonstrations, and promotion of advanced concrete and asphalt airfield pavement technologies through grants and cooperative agreements with universities and nonprofits.
Requires the FAA to begin a rulemaking within 180 days to extend noncommercial general aviation aircraft registration periods from three to five years.
Narrows aircraft owner/lessor liability by removing the restriction that limits it to incidents occurring on land or water and clarifying it applies only when the owner lacks operational control.

Directs the GAO to study illegal drugs seized by federal authorities at U.S. international airports and report findings, including drug types, origins, and seizure methods, to Congress within 180 days.
Requires the GAO to report to Congress within one year on how drug traffickers use unmanned aircraft systems to smuggle illegal drugs across U.S. international borders.

Expresses Congress's view that the Secretaries of Transportation and Agriculture should work with the World Health Organization to develop guidance for preventing disease-carrying insects from being transported on commercial aircraft.
Requires the FAA to update its regulations so that long-term lessees of large or turbine-powered multiengine aircraft are treated the same as owners under FAA joint ownership policies.

Requires the FAA Administrator to report to Congress within 180 days on the agency's existing STEM outreach efforts aimed at inspiring elementary and secondary students to pursue aviation careers and address projected shortages of pilots and aviation professionals.

Directs the FAA to launch a pilot program allowing operators of older, noisier Stage 2 aircraft to fly those planes in nonrevenue service to up to four qualifying medium hub or nonhub airports, subject to facility and operational requirements, with the program expiring in 10 years or when no Stage 2 aircraft remain in service.

Requires the Secretary of Transportation to publish a proposed rule within one year—and a final rule within 18 months thereafter—establishing noise and operational standards to permit civil supersonic flight over land in the United States, with the existing ban on overland supersonic flight automatically voided if the final rule is not published within 36 months.

Allows FAA-regulated air carriers to fly to destinations under visual flight rules without a Terminal Aerodrome Forecast or METAR if an Area Forecast and an alternate airport with those reports are available, and prohibits the FAA from imposing stricter requirements without objective, evidence-based safety justification.
Makes a series of minor technical corrections—such as fixing cross-references, capitalization, spelling errors, and punctuation—in various sections of aviation and tax law.

Directs the FAA to publish weather observation data from National Weather Service stations and automated weather stations located in noncontiguous states through the FAA's National Airspace Data Interchange Network.
Expands the duties of the National Advisory Committee on Travel and Tourism Infrastructure to include considering the impact of domestic and international aviation markets on U.S. travel and tourism.
Expresses Congress's view that the aviation industry should actively encourage women and girls to pursue aviation careers through pilot training, STEM education, and mentorship programs.

States Congress's findings about the Spectrum Efficient National Surveillance Radar (SENSR) program and expresses the sense of Congress that the FAA should continue assessing whether the 1300–1350 MHz spectrum band can be made available for non-Federal use.

Requires the FAA Administrator to report to Congress within 180 days analyzing a decade of illegal charter flight reports, including FAA enforcement actions, resource allocation decisions, challenges in identifying illegal operators, and recommendations for improvement.

Prohibits states, their subdivisions, and multi-state authorities from enacting or enforcing meal or rest break rules for commercial truck drivers whose hours of service are already regulated by the federal government.

summary: This bill reauthorizes the Federal Aviation Administration through fiscal year 2021, appropriating funds for airport improvement, air navigation, FAA operations, and research and development. It also comprehensively reforms FAA safety oversight, certification, and consumer protection rules, establishes a regulatory framework for unmanned aircraft systems (drones), modernizes the NextGen air traffic control system, and addresses general aviation, essential air service, and various other aviation policy matters.
